questions.question: Explain the components of a page table entry (PTE) and their purposes.
keyPoints: Frame number: indicates physical location of the page
Valid/Invalid bit: shows if page is resident in memory
Protection bits: specify read/write/execute permissions
Dirty bit: indicates if page has been modified
Reference bit: tracks if page has been recently accessed
Other optional bits: caching, user/supervisor mode

questions.question: What are the advantages of a hierarchical (multi-level) page table over a single-level page table?
keyPoints: Reduces memory overhead by allocating only needed page tables
Handles sparse address spaces efficiently
Outer page table covers large virtual address range
Inner tables allocated only for valid virtual memory
Scales well for large virtual address spaces

questions.question: Describe the working of a hashed page table and its suitability for large address spaces.
keyPoints: Uses hash function to map virtual page numbers to entries
Hash table indexed by virtual page number
Each bucket contains chain of entries for collision handling
Efficient for address spaces larger than physical memory
Commonly used in systems with 64-bit addressing

questions.question: What is an inverted page table? Explain its advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: One entry per physical frame, indexed by frame number
Entry contains owning process ID and virtual page number
Advantage: memory efficient for large virtual address spaces
Disadvantage: slower translation, difficulty in implementing shared memory
Reduces page table size from O(virtual memory) to O(physical memory)

questions.question: Explain the role of the Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) in address translation.
keyPoints: Hardware cache storing recent virtual-to-physical translations
Associative memory for fast parallel lookup
Contains virtual page number and corresponding physical frame
On TLB miss: page table accessed in memory, TLB updated
On TLB hit: direct translation without memory access
Improves memory access performance significantly

questions.question: Calculate the size of a page table for a system with 32-bit virtual addresses, 4KB page size, and 4-byte page table entries. Also explain why this may be problematic.
keyPoints: Page table size = 1,048,576 × 4 = 4,194,304 bytes = 4 MB
For each process, 4MB page table is required
Problematic for systems with many processes
Solution: hierarchical page tables to allocate only needed entries

questions.question: Discuss in detail the various page table structures used in modern operating systems. Compare them in terms of memory efficiency, access time, and suitability for different system configurations.
keyPoints: Single-level/Flat page table: simple but memory-inefficient for large address spaces
Hierarchical/Multi-level: reduces memory by allocating only needed tables, uses multiple levels
Inverted page table: one entry per physical frame, space-efficient for large virtual addresses
Hashed page table: uses hash function for efficient lookup, handles large address spaces
Comparison: hierarchical best for moderate spaces, inverted for large sparse spaces, hashed for very large address spaces
Trade-offs: hierarchical adds translation overhead, inverted complicates sharing, hashed needs good hash function

questions.question: Explain the address translation process in a two-level page table system with a numerical example. Also discuss the advantages of this scheme.
keyPoints: Virtual address divided into: outer page index, inner page index, offset
Example: 32-bit VA, 4KB pages = 12-bit offset
Remaining 20 bits split: 10 bits outer, 10 bits inner
Translation: outer index → outer PTE → inner table base, inner index → PTE → frame number
Combine frame number with offset for physical address
Advantages: reduced memory usage, handles sparse address space, scalable

questions.question: What is a page fault? Explain the steps involved in handling a page fault by the operating system. Also discuss how page table entries are updated during this process.
keyPoints: Page fault occurs when valid bit is 0 during memory access
Steps: 1) Trap to OS, 2) Determine if valid reference, 3) Find free frame or evict victim, 4) Load page from secondary storage, 5) Update page table entry, 6) Restart instruction
PTE updates: set frame number, set valid bit, set protection bits, clear dirty bit
If page was modified, write to disk before loading new page
TLB also updated with new translation

questions.question: Discuss the various page replacement algorithms used in operating systems and explain how the page table assists these algorithms.
keyPoints: Algorithms: FIFO, Optimal, LRU, LRU-Approximation (Clock/Second Chance)
FIFO: simple queue-based, may suffer from Belady's anomaly
Optimal: replaces pages not used for longest time, not implementable
LRU: approximates optimal, uses reference bits and timestamps
Clock algorithm: uses reference bit in circular list, practical implementation
Page table assists: reference bit shows recent access, dirty bit shows modification
Victim selection: reference bit helps identify pages to evict
